summary: DHH responds to a user's criticism of Basecamp's design decision to algorithmically determine project status rather than using manual user input. He defends 37signals' approach of launching simple software and iterating based on real-world feedback, arguing that trying to anticipate every feature need upfront leads to analysis paralysis. The post frames the missing feature not as an oversight but as a natural consequence of betting on simplicity.
key_points: Pursuing simplicity means placing bets on which features matter, and some bets will inevitably be wrong
Over-dramatizing missing features ignores the intentional tradeoff behind launching lean software
Analysis paralysis in pursuit of complete information makes companies boring and slow
Post-launch iteration is a natural evolution of understanding, not a sign of incompetence
money_quotes: When you're trying to archive such a goal, it's the same as placing a number of bets with varying odds.
There's no way we can (or could afford to) asses or even contemplate how every single feature is going to play out in advance.
So some of the bets are bound to turn out bad.
Does it mean we should be struck by analysis paralysis in order to archive complete information about every bet?
What an extremely boring and slow company 37signals would be then.
key_insight: Launching simple software means accepting that some design bets will be wrong, and that's preferable to the paralysis of trying to anticipate every user need upfront.
